items.title: Center and scale deliberately
items.avoid: Running PCA on raw features without deciding whether scale should matter.
items.prefer: Center always; add StandardScaler when units differ and correlation-style PCA is intended.
items.why: Centering is required; scaling determines whether high-variance features dominate components.
items.example: Pipeline([('scale', StandardScaler()), ('pca', PCA())]) for mixed units; PCA() alone on already comparable features.

items.title: Fit on training data only
items.avoid: Calling fit_transform on the concatenation of train and test before cross-validation.
items.prefer: Nest PCA in CV pipelines; fit on each training fold, transform validation folds.
items.why: Global fit leaks test distribution into components and optimistic metrics.
items.example: cross_val_score(Pipeline([('pca', PCA(5)), ('svc', SVC())]), X_train, y_train, cv=5).

items.title: Inspect explained variance before fixing k
items.avoid: Hard-coding k=2 because plots are two-dimensional.
items.prefer: Plot cumulative explained variance; try task-driven k via validation or `PCA(n_components=0.95)`.
items.why: k should balance compression against information retained for your goal.
items.example: print(pca.explained_variance_ratio_.cumsum()) and mark the smallest k reaching 0.90 cumulative.

items.title: Read loadings, not just scatter plots
items.avoid: Interpreting PC axes without checking which original features contribute.
items.prefer: Examine `components_` rows to see feature weights on each axis.
items.why: A component is a mixture; loadings explain what variance direction means in domain terms.
items.example: For component 0, argsort(abs(pca.components_[0])) lists the strongest contributing feature indices.
